workshop_short_name: Workshop on Frontiers of Optimization for Robotics, 2nd Edition
talk_title: Numerical Optimal Control for Nonsmooth Systems
speaker: Christian Dietz and Moritz Diehl
tc_relevance_reason: Diehl's group builds acados, the open-source toolkit that powers fast nonlinear MPC on real robots; if you run real-time MPC, you likely depend on his methods. This talk pushes nonlinear optimal control into nonsmooth dynamics, which is exactly what's needed to plan and control through contact.
workshop_tc_importance: This workshop is centered on numerical optimization methods for robotics — trajectory optimization, differentiable physics, learning-augmented optimal control, and global solvers. Every theme is direct TC scope: these are the underlying methods behind modern motion planning, MPC, and contact-aware control. The speakers and tooling that come up here are the same ones the TC's planning and control work builds on.

workshop_short_name: Workshop on Frontiers of Optimization for Robotics, 2nd Edition
talk_title: A gap penalty semismooth method for QPCC with applications to contact-implicit trajectory optimization
speaker: Toshiyuki Ohtsuka
tc_relevance_reason: QPCCs (quadratic programs with complementarity constraints) are the math behind contact-implicit trajectory optimization. Ohtsuka wrote a standard textbook on nonlinear MPC and pioneered C/GMRES real-time MPC. This talk gives a new solver for the contact-mode problem that defines a lot of TC research.
workshop_tc_importance: This workshop is centered on numerical optimization methods for robotics — trajectory optimization, differentiable physics, learning-augmented optimal control, and global solvers. talk_title: TBD

Hearing him directly is the closest thing to an authoritative update on the toolchain TC members use daily.

workshop_short_name: Workshop on Frontiers of Optimization for Robotics, 2nd Edition
talk_title: Beyond Discrete-Time MPC: Nonlinear Lifting and Intersample Optimization in Robotics
speaker: Kaoru Yamamoto (remote)
tc_relevance_reason: Standard MPC discretizes time and ignores what happens between sample points, which matters more and more as robots get faster. This talk addresses that gap with continuous-time / intersample formulations — relevant niche for anyone whose controller runs at a different rate than their planner.

talk_title: Non-smooth control for MuJoCo physics
speaker: Emo Todorov
tc_relevance_reason: Todorov is the original author of MuJoCo and a foundational figure on iLQR/iLQG who rarely gives public talks anymore. Hearing the creator of the simulator most TC researchers run their planners and controllers against, presenting his own non-smooth control work inside that simulator, is essentially impossible to get anywhere else.

workshop_short_name: Workshop on Frontiers of Optimization for Robotics, 2nd Edition
talk_title: Graphs of Convex Sets: A New Framework for Efficient Discrete-Continuous Optimization
speaker: Tobia Marcucci
tc_relevance_reason: Graphs of Convex Sets (GCS) is the most influential new motion-planning formulation of the last few years — it lets you solve mixed discrete-continuous problems (like choosing a homotopy class AND a smooth trajectory) as a single convex program. Hearing the originator explain it at the exact moment the field is adopting it is genuinely time-sensitive.

workshop_short_name: Workshop on Frontiers of Optimization for Robotics, 2nd Edition
talk_title: Scaling Semidefinite Relaxations for Robot Perception and Control
speaker: Heng Yang
tc_relevance_reason: SDP relaxations give certifiable global optima for nonconvex problems but have historically been too slow for robotics. This talk explicitly pushes the framework from perception into control — useful for TC members curious about global-optimality guarantees in trajectory or pose optimization.

workshop_short_name: Riemann and Gauss meet Asimov: 3rd Tutorial on Geometric Methods in Robot Learning, Optimization and Control
talk_title: Tutorial session: Riemannian latent variable models
speaker: Organizers (tutorial)
tc_relevance_reason: Pedagogical session on Riemannian latent variable models — generative ML on manifolds. Useful background for TC members exploring manifold-aware learned policies, but not direct planning/control methodology.
workshop_tc_importance: Geometric methods — Lie groups, Riemannian optimization, manifold-aware learning — underpin the TC's treatment of pose, motion, and control on SE(3) and other matrix Lie groups. This tutorial covers the mathematical foundations that get used across trajectory optimization, geometric control, and structured policy learning. It pairs naturally with Friday's 'Geometry in the Age of Data-Driven Robotics' workshop.

workshop_short_name: Workshop on the Path Towards Generalizable Contact-Rich Robotics: Control and Representation
talk_title: Panel 1: Contact Model Representations
speaker: Nima Fazeli, Marc Toussaint, Yuval Tassa, Yilun Du
tc_relevance_reason: Discussion of how to represent contact (analytical vs learned, rigid vs compliant, differentiable simulators). Useful upstream context for TC algorithms but the panel itself is about representations rather than planning/control methodology.
workshop_tc_importance: Contact-rich robotics sits at the intersection of control through hybrid dynamics, MPC through contact, and representation learning for manipulation. The workshop covers how planners and controllers reason about the discontinuities that classical smooth-dynamics methods struggle with. Highly relevant to TC members working on manipulation, legged locomotion, and whole-body control.

talk_title: Panel 2: Algorithms for Contact-Rich Control
speaker: Justin Carpentier, Bibit Bianchini, Hae-Won Park, Emo Todorov
tc_relevance_reason: Explicitly titled 'Algorithms for Contact-Rich Control' — the most TC-targeted single hour at Monday's workshops. Four researchers whose collective work (Pinocchio, contact-implicit trajopt at UPenn, KAIST whole-body legged control, MuJoCo/iLQR) probably sits inside the toolchain of every TC member doing contact-rich planning or control. Pairing Carpentier and Todorov on the same panel is rare.

talk_title: Distributionally Robust Control via Stein Variational Inference for Contact-Rich Manipulation
speaker: Hrishikesh Sathyanarayan, Victor Vantilborgh, Harish Ravichandar, Tom Lefebvre, Ian Abraham
tc_relevance_reason: Stein variational inference for distributionally robust control under contact uncertainty. Direct TC algorithmic contribution: extends MPC to handle model error in a principled way.

talk_title: Don't Break the Egg: Branch-Rejoining Trajectory Optimization under Contact Timing Uncertainties
speaker: Zhuocheng Zhang, Haizhou Zhao, Xudong Sun, Aaron M. Johnson, Majid Khadiv
tc_relevance_reason: Direct attack on a core open problem in contact-implicit trajectory optimization: contact timing is inherently uncertain, so this work proposes a branch-rejoining trajopt that explicitly handles that uncertainty. TC algorithmic contribution.

speaker: Grey Sarmiento, Michael Posa
tc_relevance_reason: Posa's group dissecting which components of contact-implicit MPC actually drive performance on manipulation tasks. Empirical TC methodology paper that informs how to deploy these methods correctly.

workshop_short_name: Workshop on the Path Towards Generalizable Contact-Rich Robotics: Control and Representation
talk_title: Panel 3: State and Future for Contact-Rich Control
speaker: Matt Mason, Aaron Ames, Aaron Johnson, Dmitry Berenson
tc_relevance_reason: Closing panel by senior figures of contact-rich manipulation and legged locomotion. Mason wrote the textbook on mechanics of manipulation (literally — Mechanics of Robotic Manipulation, MIT Press) and rarely panels anymore; Ames invented control barrier functions for safe locomotion; Johnson works on legged contact dynamics; Berenson on planning under contact uncertainty. Senior-figure reflection on where contact-rich planning/control is going.
workshop_tc_importance: Contact-rich robotics sits at the intersection of control through hybrid dynamics, MPC through contact, and representation learning for manipulation. The workshop covers how planners and controllers reason about the discontinuities that classical smooth-dynamics methods struggle with. Highly relevant to TC members working on manipulation, legged locomotion, and whole-body control.

workshop_short_name: 1st Workshop on Generalization in Autonomous Driving: Paradigms Practice and Public Road Demonstrations
talk_title: Distinguished Talk 4
speaker: Marco Pavone
tc_relevance_reason: Pavone's Stanford ASL is one of the few AV research groups publishing deeply on decision-making, motion planning, and safe control in the classical TC tradition. Recent work spans GCS-based AV planning, reachability-based safety, and contingency planning. In a workshop otherwise dominated by end-to-end learned-driving talks, his is the planning/control voice.
workshop_tc_importance: Autonomous driving is a major application domain for TC research — motion planning, MPC, prediction-aware control, and decision-making under uncertainty all show up in AV stacks. This workshop's emphasis is on generalization and on-road deployment rather than algorithm design, so the TC connection is at the systems and evaluation level rather than the methods level.
